Ma-PICKENS—Mi Chairman, with
the indulgence of the committee, I will
in a few words present you with my
views upon this question. The highest argument with me in fa­
vor of the bill, is in the intrinsic justice
of the principle it contains, and its con­
sistency with our own professions on the
subject o

sistency with our own professions on the
subject of impressment. This will ap­
pear evident from a fair vie'w of the
point of difference between us and our
enemy on • this subject. What is that;
point ? It is this-—our government has
ever contended that its flag should pro­
tect the seamen employed under it; G
Britain asserts the right of seizing and
impressing from on board our vessels
t her own subjeete - Our position is sup­
ported by two concMtive r r. »o»s: 1st-
As the flag represents the sovereignty
of the nation to which it belongs, any
violence on

of the nation to which it belongs, any
violence on those over whom it waves
is through it an act of war on the nation­
al sovereignty, and incompatible with a j state of dignified peace. 2d. The pow
er of making impressments from our
vessels, is from its very nature liable to
constant and serious abuse, which our
experience has too strongly prov d in
the thousands of our fcitizvna,native as
well as naturalized, who have been seiz
ed as British subjects, and are now com­
pelled to fight against their country.
This reason

pelled to fight against their country.
This reason urges itself mere forcibly
as respects Britain and America, whose
inhabitants have the same language and
manners, than it docs between any other
countries of the world, most of which
have striking peculiarities from which
their seamen may be, distinguished.
Whatever propriety, therefore, there exercise of the power which England
practice of impressment** as relates
to other countries, reason and ex­
perience unite in declaring that, as re­
spects America, it cannot be tolerated. If, then,

spects America, it cannot be tolerated. If, then, our principle be correct, that
the flag is to be vewed as the only cri­
terion between our seamen and those of
Britain ; if it is to be a sanctuary to all
persons employed under it, justice re­
quires that those so employed should
be rightfully there-—otherwise we would
act unjustly and inconsistently with our
own principles. If we admit as seamen
persons who are the rightful property of
another state which may want them, and
give them the protection of our flag,
our governme

our government becomes directly an
instrument ol injustice and injury, to
such other state, and will be bound in

has a rigi-t to the service of it6 citizens

his new stat

presentative sovereignty of an indepen­
dent nation, its dominion or its flag. To

it is therefore oiir duty to exclude from

milar regulation, and desisting from im­

bill—Why adopt this measure now ?
The time forms a greater objection with
many than the .substance of the measure.
From this

many than the .substance of the measure.
From this it has been viewed as a con­
cession to the enemy—a retreat from the

of difference narrowed down to the
single subject of impressment. This

power,. defined the great qU' Stron in

style an act of justice which a nation

disposition and relation, which happily subsists
between his Royal Highness the Prince Regent
of Po

between his Royal Highness the Prince Regent
of Portugal and the government of the United
States." I have the honor to remain, with due respect
and consideration, your Excellency 's most obe­
dient servant. (Signed) HENRY (HI L, Consul.
To liis Excellency, the Count Dot Arcos, Capt. Gen.and Gov. of the province &
city of St. Salvadore, &c. &c. Ac. St. Sulvudorc, 3d Jan. 1813.
SIR—I have the satisfaction to participate to
your excellency that Com. Bainbridge having
become acquainted through me of the interest
manif

become acquainted through me of the interest
manifested by your excellency for the libera
tion of Ll Gen. Hislop and his staff, has re­
solved to take upon himself the responsibility
of paroling those officers here, as well as the Commander of the late British frigate Java, on
board which they were captured, and Captain
Marshall, a supernumerary marine officer, in
the service of his Britannic Majesty—taken al­
so by the Commander on board that frigate. In this act of liberality on the part of Com
Bainbridge, your excellency will only-perceive
the

Bainbridge, your excellency will only-perceive
the friendly and generous; spirit by which he
is actuated, in desiring to correspond as far
as in his power with the wishes of your excel­
lency, and to mitigate t je evil arising out of
the event of war, from tile misfortunes of indi­
viduals, whom a brave Commander will never
recognize as the enemies of his country, in the
character of prisoners of war. I have the honor to remain, with sentiments
of respect and consideration, your excellency's
most ob't servant, (Signed) HENRY HILL, Consul
THE

most ob't servant, (Signed) HENRY HILL, Consul
THE REPLY,.
The Count Dos Arcos presents his compli
ments to Mr. Hill, requesting him to make
known to Commodore Bainbridge his very sin­
cere acknowledgments . for the beneficence
which he has had the goodness to shew toward
Lieutenant-General Hislop and the other officer
passengers in the Java- -.profiting of this occa­
sion to r.-new to Mr. Hill the sentiment of his
consideration and esteem. COJUMOPORE BAINBRIDGE TO THE
SECRETARY OF THE NAVY. I have the honor qf enclosi

SECRETARY OF THE NAVY. I have the honor qf enclosing to j
you a copy of a correspondence, which '
passed between the American and Bri j
tish Consuls at St. Salvadore, relative
to a challenge offered by captain Law­
rence, commander of the United States'
ship Hornet, and refused by Captain
Green, commander of his B. M. ship
Bonne Citoyenne, a vessel in size and
force greater than the Hornet. Captain
Green's excuse I have no doubt will be
viewed by those who see it in its pro­
per light. He certainly was not war­
ranted in que

per light. He certainly was not war­
ranted in questioning the sacred pledge
I made to him- The confidence I had
in the gallant commander, the brave of­
ficers and crew qf the Hornet, (all of
whom exhibited the most ardent desire
for the contest) induced me to take the
responsibility qf the pledge, from which
I certainly shotild never have swerved;
and the strongest proof I can give of
that confidence, is leaving the Hornet
four days together off the harbor in
which the Bonne Citoyenne laid, anil
from which she could discover that the
Constitutio

from which she could discover that the
Constitution was'not within 40 miles of
it; therefore at atty period capt. Green'
could have been certain of contending
with her alone j finally, to prevent his
having the least possible excuse, I went
into the harbor qf St. Salvador and laid
three days, where he could have de­
tained fne 34 hours on application to
the Governor; these three' days the
Hornet remained off the harbour, and
the Bonne Citoyenne continued safely
at anchor. On my leaving the coast of Brazils, I
l

at anchor. On my leaving the coast of Brazils, I
left captain Lawrence to watch her, and
have no doubt should he fall in with
her, that the result will be honorable to
his country and t Having stated to
you, sir, mere' facts, 1 now beg leave
to observe, that I consider the refusal
of captain Green to meet the Hornet, as
a victory gained by the latter vessel.
Our enemy (who are brave) in the vic­
tories which we have obtained over
them, have attributed them to our hav­
ing superior force,' when in fact the
difference of force has not been com­
parable with

difference of force has not been com­
parable with the superiority of effect
done by us; but in the present instance
they have not the least shade of such
colouring, for the Bonne Citoyenne is
a larger vessel and greater force in
guns and men than the Hornet, but the
high State qf discipline and excellent
order which the Hornet is in, makes
me feel confident of a favorable result
in the issue of an action between them.
Permit me, sir, to take this opportuni­
ty of expressing to you the great satis­
faction I have received from Captain
Lawrence's co

faction I have received from Captain
Lawrence's conduct in every instance
since being under my command, and I
respectfully recommend him particu­
larly to your notice as a most meritori­
ous officer. I have the honor, sir, to be, with the
greatest respect, your obedient hum­
ble servant. Wm. BAINBRIDGE.
Hon. Secretary of the JVavy St. Salvadore, 28<A Dec. 1813.
SIR—The following is an ex.ract from a let­

.the U. States ship Hornet, now off this port.
1 * When I last saw you, 1 stated to you m, wish
t

1 * When I last saw you, 1 stated to you m, wish
to meet the Bonne Citoyenne, and authorised
ou to make my wishes known to Capt. Green,
now request you to state to him, that I will
meet him whenever he may be pleased to come
out, and pledge my honor that the Constitution
nor-any other Anu-ican vessels shall interfere." Commodore Bainbridge of the Constitution
frigate, confirms to me the request of Captain
Lawrence, in these words—" if Capt. Green
wishes to try equal force, I pledge my honor to
give-him an opportunity by being out of the
way, o

give-him an opportunity by being out of the
way, or net Interfering." In communicating these sentiments to you,
sir, of Com. Bainbridge and Capt Lawrence,
with a request tliatypu will participate them
to Capt. Green of Kb Britannic Majesty's ship
Bonne Citoyenne, iWh in this port, I trust you
will perceive no motive on their part or on
mine, that does not result from national hostili­
ty, and the honor of our respective countries—
or that ought m any respect to weaken the
bonds or diminish the regard of private or soci­
a

bonds or diminish the regard of private or soci­
al duties—and that you will believe I have, ac­
cording to my sincere wish and best judgement,
in conveying through you to Capt. Green the re­
quest of Capt. Lawrence, been guitkd by those
feelings of delicacy on this occasion and regard
for the meritorious character of Capt. Green,
with which the slight acquaintance formed here
with that gentleman has inspired me. I am sir, very respectfully, your most obedi­
ent servant, (Signed)
HENRY HILL, Consul.
To Frederick Linderman, Si..Salvadore, 39<A Dec. 1

To Frederick Linderman, Si..Salvadore, 39<A Dec. 1812.
Dear Sir—Since writing my letter of this
evening, which will be handed you with this
by Capt. Davis, I have received an answer from
Mr. Linderman, the British Consul, communi­
cating Capt. Green's reception of your chal­
lenge, which I transcribe. " Fort de St. Pedro, 29th Dec. 1812.
Sir—I transmitted your letter of yesterday
to Capt. P. B. Green, to whom the substance
is directed, and haying received his reply, I
herewith insert it «er6af»s»—" I hasten to ac­
kno

herewith insert it «er6af»s»—" I hasten to ac­
knowledge the favor of your communication,
made to me this morning, from Mr. HUI, Con­
sul of the U. S. of America, on the subject of
a challenge stated to have been offered through
Mr. Hill by Captain Lawrence, of the U. States'
sloop of war Hornet, to my.-elf, as Commander
of H B. M. ship Bonne Citoyenne, anchored in
this port, pledging his honor, as well as that
of Com. Bainbridge, that no advantage shall be
taken by the Constitution or any other Ameri­
can vessel whatever on the occasion. I am
convince

can vessel whatever on the occasion. I am
convinced, sir, if such a rencontre was to take
pladsj the result could not be long dubious,
and would terminate favorably to the ship
which I have the honor to confmand; blit I am
equally convinced that Com. Bainbridge could
not swerve so much from the paramount duty
he owes to his country, as to Become an inac­
tive spectator, and see a ship belonging to the
very squadron under h>> orders fall into the
hands of an enemy—this reason operates pow­
erfully on my mind for not exposing the Bonne
Cito

erfully on my mind for not exposing the Bonne
Citoyenne to a risk upon terms so manifestly • disadvantageous, as those proposed by Com.
Bainbridge—indeed nothing would give me
more satisfaction than cofnplying. with the
wishes of Capt. Lawrence, and I earnestly hope
that chance will afford him an opportunity of
meeting the Bonne Citoyenpe, under different
circumstances, to enable him to distinguish .
himself in the manner he is now so desirous of
doing. I further assure, that my ship will at
all times be prepared, wherever she may be, to.
re

all times be prepared, wherever she may be, to.
repel any attack- made against her, and I shall
also act offensively whenever I shall judge pro­
per to do 80." You have here, sir, Capt. Green's senti­
ments, and, with Com. Bainbridge, will form
such opinion of them as they merit. On send­
ing off the supplies required by the Commo­
dore; I shall write you more fully, and shall be I
full in my communications to him also, which '
I pray you will mention to him, and accept the
renewed assurances of real esteem, With which I re

renewed assurances of real esteem, With which I remain your most ob't servt.
(Signed) HENRY HILL, Consul •

Mr. Quincy from the select commit­

th

fines, penalties and forfeitures accruing under

and mitigaltog -powffwj ewtroisod by thoTrea-

cretion. In some commercial communities a
similar discretion is invested in a board of

a check upon each other, and the publicity of
whose proceedings preserve'their decisions un­
der

necessary. The bill making appropriations for

licence bill; which having been further

fora third reading. The report of the committee of

fora third reading. The report of the committee of Fo­

the principle of retaliation, but arose ,
from the

the principle of retaliation, but arose ,

from sovereignty* The amendments were ordered to be

in the chair, on .the bill prohibitiag the

will be covered with them. The fruit of
the tree constitutes the principal food of
the natives one half of the year. Thirty
or forty trees will support a family. Tho
tree often grows to the height of more than
100 feet without branches, and the trunk
through, this extent -continuing of an uni»
form size, being from twelve to fifteen or <
eighteen inches in diameter. The fruit
grows among the large leaves, which form
a circle of ten or twelve feet in diameter
around

a circle of ten or twelve feet in diameter
around the top. The cocoa-nut tree grows
and bears fruit in a similar maimer, except j
that the leaves are much longer, a single I
leaf being commonly twelve or fifteen feet | j in lcngtA._jrhe_cecoa.BUt does not «r.o^
kso erect, as
is. muc^ghte^, g.——• jail. cabbage with long leaves That has no
head, will give you some idea of the shape
. of these curious and very useful trees. Extract from the Journal of Messrs.W ar-
ren and Poor, .American Missionaries
at Tillipally in the Inland of Ceylon.
Sabbath,Oct

at Tillipally in the Inland of Ceylon.
Sabbath,Oct.20,1816.—D. Bast,Esq spent
the day with us. In the morning about 30
people came to the bouse to hear what we
had to say. We explained to them the
object of our coming to dwell among them,
and the manner in which we should pro­
ceed in accomplishing it. They professed
to be well pleased with our intentions, and
expressed a wish to have their children in­
structed. At the suggestion of Mr. Bast,
we appointed a meeting to. be held next
Sabbath at Mallagum, two miles distant
toward Jaff

Sabbath at Mallagum, two miles distant
toward Jaffaapatam in Mr.B's. court house. —
This gentleman, a native of Dutch descent,
is a sitting magistrate, and holds bis court
at Mallagum. His jurisdiction extends
over fourteen parishes in this district- Ho
appears to be a truly pious man ; takes a
lively interest in our settling here, and we
think will afford us much assistance in our
missionary work. Sabbath 27.—This morning sixty per­
sons came to us. We held worship in
our house. Sung in English, prayed in
Malabar, by

our house. Sung in English, prayed in
Malabar, by the assistapee of an interpre­
ter, and expounded the 2d chapter of St.
Matthew's Gospel, concluded with singing,^
and prayer. After the services we l>a<s|
some cawxwwiwwt ....y*w:—tl ojiSg*
were ready to acknowledge that what we i
had said to them was good, and expressed
a wish that we might preach to them eve­
ry Sabbath. We are aware that very un­
worthy motives may induce this people to
come to us; but we are gratified in having ; an op

come to us; but we are gratified in having ; an opportunity of opening to'them the
treasures of the Gospel, hoping that in due I time, God will bless the means of his own
j appointment. In consequence of heavy
' rains we had no meeting at Mallagum. In i the afternoon held worship in the family,
i Wednesday 30. We see some good cf-
' fectsofthe Rev. Mr. Palm's exertions a-
, mong this people, though he labored un­
der great disadvantages. The prejudices
of the people are in' some degree removed;
some knowledge of Christianity was com­
municated,

some knowledge of Christianity was com­
municated, arid here are ten or twelve boys
who attended his school, now wishing to
be instructed by us. Though we are not
ready to open a school, yet as these boys
are daily with us, we have given them pas­
sages of Scripture written on ollas, which
they commit with surprising facility.—
they are also learning the English alphabet. Tuesday 12. Engaged in studying the
Tamul language. We much need the
assistance of some one who is acquainted
with the grammar and principles of the
language. S

with the grammar and principles of the
language. Such a person it is extremely
difficult, if not impossible, to obtain on this
.. island. We have one set of elementary
l', books. Books of this description cannot
be purchased here. On the continent they
I are scarce, and excessively dear. Malle-
i appa, our interpreter, is able to assist us in
l construing and pronouncing the language.
, He is the son of a native clergyman, is pre-
I paring for the ministry, and we think,
i gives some evidence of possessing the es-
f sent

i gives some evidence of possessing the es-
f sential qualifications.
. This day we made our first visits among
. the natives. We visited two sick women;
1 the one a poor widow, the mother of an ac­
tive intelligent little boy, who has expresS-
n ed an earnest desire to come and write pas-
d sages of Scripture with the other boys, but
i was under the necessity of using every ex-
n ertion with his two brothers, to procure
it rice for the family. After proper inquiry,
o we concluded to give him two measures-
nearly tw

o we concluded to give him two measures-
nearly two quarts of rice, worth four cents
s per week, which will enable him to devote
d his time to study. We also gave him a
Is cubit of cloth, which serves for a full dress,
is which he very much needed. He did not
e need a knowledge of our languge to ex-
y press to us his feelings. His countenance
i. and gestures manifested the gladness of
e his heart, in being placed in sudb favorable
e circumstances. On visiting this boy's
e mother, we were pleased to.see, suspended
d from

e mother, we were pleased to.see, suspended
d from the roof of the cottage, a number of
s ollas on which were written the passages
is of Scripture which the other boys had
e learned, and which this had transcribed,
h On asking the woman if she had any know*
it ledge of Christianity, she replied, that she
3, knew only what her son had read to her
e from the ollas. The other woman whom
e we visited was a nerson Who had sent th arq a church, a dwelling house, and five
other small buildings, two yards, a garden,
four w

other small buildings, two yards, a garden,
four wells, eleven margosa trees, and fifty-
one palmyra trees; all belonging to the
Ceylon government. That you may/khow
in what state we found them, we give a
short description of them. 1. The church. This is said to be the
finest church in the whole district, land in
the best state of repair. It was first built
by the Portuguese, when they were mas­
ters of the island in the sixteenth century,
(as were all these churches and houses,)
and repaired by the-Dutch in I678^as ap­
pears by

and repaired by the-Dutch in I678^as ap­
pears by the date on the -western 'fend of
the building. This is the reason why it is 1795-6, the church and houses were niuesrr'
neglected, and have been since fast going,
to decay. Many of the walls are much
broken by the banians which grow, in them, -
and break them down. This church has.,

•4 Bear Hunt.—The following account of a
Bear Hunt affords an instance of persevering in­
trepidity rarely to be met with in any individual.

trepidity rarely to be met with in any individual. On the 29th January, a bear was caught and
taken from its den, in Warwick Mountain, after
a vigilant search of eight days,by a party of hunts­
men from Warwick. The bear had taken shelter
in a declivity of rocks of about forty feet depth.
Attempts were first made to get him out by
smoaking, &c. but without effect. Dogs were
then sent into the hole, but they eitlier retreated
at his terrific aspect,or were destroyed by his grasp.
,At length the Huntsmen finding all attempts to
fr

,At length the Huntsmen finding all attempts to
frighten him from his retreat fruitless, blew the
rocks over the hole, and came within about 16 feet
of the bear. These continued operations made
him fierce and terrible. Alter the hole blown
through the rock was sufficiently large to admit
the'body of a man, John Ward, jr. crept into it, but without effect. He descended the second
time and shot him in the fore legs ; the bear at
each fire advanced towards the mouth of the hole,
but Ward was not to be intimidated ; he descend­
e

but Ward was not to be intimidated ; he descend­
ed again and shot the ferocious beast in one eye.
Ward was now drawn out, the hear fiercely fol­
lowing him; he instantly seized a rifle from the
hands of another Huntsman, and discharged its
contents into the head of the animal which proved
fatal. He measured six feet from the nose to the
end of the tail, and weighed three hundred and
thirteen pounds. . Nkw-York, Feb. '1.

ed condition, and feeling your dependence on him, : Gr
as your only Lord, Redeemer and Preserver.
ted, and the adversary will say that you mtist del
drown sorrow and disregard futurity ; but, my ' flo*
dear friend, hark ! Eternity is at the door, and if 1 111
you listen to his counsel you must soon meet his t of
horrid yell in the regions of despair. O then,can

horrid yell in the regions of despair. O then,can fre
you not even now, at this late hour, ground the 1 Of
weapons of your rebellion, have access to the to
throne of grace, and find pardon and salvation.— | ste
Do not let such thoughts as these occupy your i an
mind, that you have been so great a sinner that ' to
tberfe is no mercy; and do not consider that be- ed
.cause your provocations have been heaven-dar- ve
ing,and your crimes of the deepest dye, that there 1 ha
is no remedy. But God says, even to you, let the i

is no remedy. But God says, even to you, let the inc
wicked forsake his ways, and the unrighteous man . ly
his thoughts, and let him return unto the Lord, 1 foi
and he will have mercy on him, and unto our | J*l
Gpd, and he will abundantly pardon.
Yon liaye had a very amiable and pious com- soi
panion, and very near and dear friends, who have i ini
weeped and mourned over you, and whose hearts ' lai
must again bleed in view of the fatal hour ; and j j«i
O, what consolation Would they derive, even a- *bu
midst their most piercing pkngs, could they have I

midst their most piercing pkngs, could they have I ve
evidence that yon had, through humble repent- I sp
ance and sovereign grace, found favor and mercy, ! rai
and that not only the dissolution of your body,but I
the red«tBp*MM>w^^reMuiT. dikSS1 B^li.-------
You must no longer read the Bible as formerly, I dr
but remember that the promises and denuncia- ' mi
tions are nothing short of the word God, and will j hi,
assuredly be realized. And O, could you be so i
happy as now to lay hold on the promises of Christ

happy as now to lay hold on the promises of Christ, I
repent, and embrace the truth, how different I
would your views be; yofc could look forward I
with calm resignation at your destiny, beholding |
every thing smiling around you,even on the brink j ?
of the grave, anisay amen to the will of Heaven ; .
and although you must appear as an example and ' 11
expire in ignominy for the injured laws of God and !
inan, yet even amidst all yoirt sufferings, through < .1
a compassionate and all gracious Redeemer, you ' 0

a compassionate and all gracious Redeemer, you ' 0
j can witness his justice, sing his praises, and be I bl
I prepared to glorify him forever.
! If God's thoughts and ways were like ours,your !
| case would indeed be desperate ; but they are as "
I much above ours as the heavens are higher than | .
I the earth, therefore with him there is plenteous ; 18
| redemption. He is plenteous in mercy to all that i
I call upon him. No higher terms are propounded !

I call upon him. No higher terms are propounded ! to you th an to the least transgressor ; for he that ;
i believe th shall be. saved. The scriptures ate full j c'
i of encouragement.
j I know that I am unworthy to advise or counsel ! rc
! you,for I am a great sinner, and deeply polluted ; I 8C
! but feeling interested for your happiness, I have I *11
! in candor and' seriousness addressed you ; and O, I  ! allotted you unto salvation, is the earnest prayer ! Dec. 20, 3817. REMARKS.

have we ! lie is a transgressor of God's law; so j J*
are we ! He must u be saved by the washing of j /
I regeneration and renewing of the Holy Ghost," I ,n
or perish ; so must we ! His time is short for this 11
i great work; so is oers f Ila must soon give am .

ing an economical farm house haa

emphaaized by the farm architect of the Department of Agriculture, who states
that the mental and physical fitneee of
the laborer· both within the house and
in the fields are vitally affected by the
building that affords the family shelter.
The average American farm home baa
failed to share in the improvements that
are every day being made in agricultural
conditions and, according to the archi-
tect, is a rebuke to our boasted civilisa-
Relatively, be says, the housewife
of a century ago with her fireplace cook- ing and

of a century ago with her fireplace cook- ing and log cabin
was better provided
for than is the housewife to-day. The most important building on a
farm is the home.
Tbe health, comfort,
and happiness of the famiiy are depend-
ent upon its construction and equip-
ment, and unless these matters are look-
ed after the sanitary dairy barn or the
economically constructed buildings for
stock are of little value.
Happiness and
conteutment in the family are as essen- tial to efficient service as improved tools
and outbuildings. many cases, a lifetime in ber "w

and outbuildings. many cases, a lifetime in ber "work-
shop" the kitcheu and tbe family rooms,
she is not, as a rule, capable of planning
a house in tbe highest degree servicea-
ble and comfortable without assistance.
Her help, however is
essential to tbe
farm architect, as tbe result of bil plans
most vitally concerns her. Iu 1910 a western farm psper, at tbe
suggestion of the Department of Agri-
culture, conducted
a competition fur
farm house plans.
About600 plans of
farm houses were submitted, not one of
which wu fu

farm houses were submitted, not one of
which wu fully satisfactory. Tbe larg-
er number insisted on some particular pet notion and emphasized a single fea-
ture to the neglect of other important
The men and women who famil-

Maine inquirer: Vol.3, No. 22 - March 13, 1827 Follow this and additional works at: https://digitalmaine.com/skowhegan_history_house_newspapers his career, as well as in the latter stages of his \
life, he showed a strong attachment to the amuse-
' ment of horse-racing; and his atientJa^^^t^ ' at

' ment of horse-racing; and his atientJa^^^t^ ' attachment to the tutf, as impartial historians we ' are bound to state that he was addicted to other I fashionable vices of the age. He liked wine—
i he loved, play—and he had other tastes—unfor­
tunately toe often indulged in, by men of all
professions, but of which the cultivation is per- : haps less excusable in many other walk^rfjife
than those of the Prince, beset fretirfnancy by
the seductions of a court—and the soldier, to'x.
whom, if his mind be not more than ordinary
intell

whom, if his mind be not more than ordinary
intellectual and moral force, habits of Hcentious*^^ i ness come fatally recommended by' those of hie
reckless associates. i close, we have merely to add—that those who
were acquainted with his character well knew
that in private life he was much respected and
beloved as an amiable, affable, kind and warm
i hearted man. He readily lent a band to distress,
i whenever it was in his power to relieve it; and
it was often observed of him, that he underwent
frequent struggles when cases came before hbt) I i

frequent struggles when cases came before hbt) I in which it was impossible for him to reconcile
' the granting of favors which would be inconsis­
tent with the rigid administration of his public i duty. In his official capacity,'his merit is at-
tested by? the state of discipline in which the
British army is now kept—-by the fairness wbb_
which honors are granted—and by the general i good conduct and ftuse of hop or which are in­
fused into the profession. In his political eon-'"'"—
duct, he was unfortunate and unhappy. The
folly wh

duct, he was unfortunate and unhappy. The
folly which prompted him to deliver an irrecov-
able judgment op a question, involving the peace / i of two great Kingdoms, and the ,ciyil;relation8:
stated cannot be forgotten. !,• above the middle size, of a robust and vig/rous
constitution, which promised a' long life. ' In hie
youth he was accounted handsome, but for many
years before, his death be.was exceedingly cor­
pulent, and his form had lost its symmetry.—
Great good nature was expressed in his open
j countenance, which, however, had by no means
an

j countenance, which, however, had by no means
an intellectual cast. His voice had little flexi­
bility, and his utterance was disagreeable. During the French revolution; the Baron of
Marvel was continually tormented, by the ap-
prehension that he should die on a scaffold. All
| the cares of his wife were employed unsuecess*-
| fully to calm his fears. He sometimes indulged
I himself with the hope, that if his birth-day pass­
ed without his being arrested, be should be de­
livered from tlie weight which pressed upon his
he

livered from tlie weight which pressed upon his
heart, and might, perhaps/ be saved. Upon
one occasion, he gazed in a fit of 'deep melan-
choly upon his son, tvho was then about two
years old, and exclaimed, •• I shall never live
Utoo see this child in male clothing," ah obser-
vation which his lady carefully treasured up in
i her memory.—The horrors of the Revolution
-ijippeared'at jei-gih'tSi di^w to aT close,'aii« the: '
(biiik-day of the Baron de Marine! arrived. Ilia
I wife was preparing a 'tittle &«si' for' him -»f

I wife was preparing a 'tittle &«si' for' him -»f
4-the-occ^wpn., and the hour of supper fixed for
enjoying it. Wishing to give her husband an
agreeable surprise, and to belie bis present­
ments, Madame de Mari vet, about eleven o'clock,
when they were just serving the dessert, left the —.
table, and returning in a few moments after with (
her son in her arms, dressed like, a sailor, «he \
gave him to her husband, whom she tenderly
embraced, at.d exclaimed, " Now you see your f"
son, my dear, in man's clothing, and your birth-
d

son, my dear, in man's clothing, and your birth-
day has already passed."—"JNot yet!"'was his .
replymidnight has not struck?' His friends
shuddered at the words, and anxiously/Turned (
their eyes upon a time-piece, the fingers'of '*
which they silently regarded, as they moved to-<
ward the wished-for hour. M. de Marivet turn-
ed pale; all who surrounded him were struck
with terror. The door opened, and gave admis­
sion to the emissaries of the Revolutionary
Committee, who*were come to seize him. M.
de la C. whom, in a letter he had advised to em­
i

de la C. whom, in a letter he had advised to em­
igrate, ha^pt taken the precaution to destroy
his papera^After his departure, the? had been
transported, among other effects to tlie house of
M. de Piepape, his grandfather. The latter had
been imprisoned on suspicion, and seals had been
placed upon the property at his house. He died
in prison; and the agents of the committee, who
were present when the seals were removed,
found, in an earthan vessel, amongst some torn,
papers which were destined to be burnt, the let­
t

papers which were destined to be burnt, the let­
ter in which Monsieur de Marivet advised M. de
C. to emigrate. This letter was his sentence of
condemnation. Monsieur de Marivet was sum­
moned before the revolutionary tribunal, con­
demned to death, and lost his head upon the
scaffold just before Thermidor.
Kkhvow college.—This Institution, which '
owes its origin to the persevering efforts of Bish­
op Chase, is located in Knox county, Ohio,
about simpniles from Mt Vernon, in a tract" of
country 'which is represented as peculiarly
charmi

country 'which is represented as peculiarly
charming. It receives its name in honor of Lord
Kenyon, who has proved himself one of its best
friends, and most zealous supporters. During?
the Bishop's late visit to England, he received,
in addition to many pleasing expressions of re­
gard for the Institution, donations amounting to-
#30,000. The American Lyceum has given an account
of a subterrauean forest, discovered not long
since, near Morlaix, in Brittany, in France, by
Count La Fruglav'e. A like discovery has also
take

Count La Fruglav'e. A like discovery has also
taken place, lately, in digging a canal near Car­
lisle, in England, where a very extensive forest
of oak trees was found under ground. The trees
are all inclining towards the north, and covered
with four feet depth of earth, and abov e a Ha­
inan wall is discernable. The wood of these
trees appears in a good state of preservation, and
differs but little from our common oak wood. It
is presumed the forest must have been a very
long time under ground, before the Romans
erected

long time under ground, before the Romans
erected the wall that stands upon it.—Lon. pa. Suiting the action to the word.—In a
recent trial for assault and battery, in Pennsyl­
vania, the counsel for the defendant asked one of
the witnesses, a stout, athletic man, to describe
the manner in which the plaintiff was assaulted;
when he immediately took hold of the counsel
by the collar and gave him a tremendous shaking,
to the no small amusement of the judges, spec­
tators, &c. who were convulseAwith laughter. On th

tators, &c. who were convulseAwith laughter. On the -27th of January] 1809, Col. Wardle'i
introduced the subject in theHouso of Commons.
He began by a modest apology for his own un-
worthiness, and then expatiated on the height to
which corruption bad risen injhe disposal of mili­
tary patronage, alluded to the improper connec­
tion which subsisted between the ( ommander in
Chief and Mrs Clarke, and submitted five cases
of corruption to the House, which he was pre­
pared to. substantiate. The first case was that of

pared to. substantiate. The first case was that of
Tonryn. He paid Mrs Clarke 5001. on being ;
promoted to the rank Of Major; or mor? W-''
rectly speaking, this sum was paid to a silver­
smith On Mrs Clarke's account, in part payment
of a service of plate which-had been orde: ed for
herself and the Duke. The second case was
that of an exchange which was effected between
two Colonels (Brooke and Knight,) and for
which Mrs Clarke received 200/. The third case
was of a differenf^escription, that two most
meritorious officers^who mutually wished to ex-
I

meritorious officers^who mutually wished to ex-
I change, Were prevented from doing so, in conse­
quence of their' inability to make the presents
I which were expected. The fourth case was
| more important than any of the former. A Ma­
jor Shaw had, after repeated negociations, ob- i tained- from Mrs G, the assurance that he should i receive the appointment of Dep. Barrack-master
at the Cape on paying her 1,0001. Heultimate-
ly received the appointment, but was enabled to
make good to Mrs Clarke no more than 000/ —
The la

make good to Mrs Clarke no more than 000/ —
The lady complained to the Cemmander-in-Cbief,
and the unhappy defaulter, instead of going out
to the Cape was kept at borne, and educed to ! French was appointed, through Mrs Clarke's in­
fluence, to conduct a levy. • He was to pay over
to her a guinea of the bounty of each man, to­
gether with the sale of patronage of commissions
to a certain extent. A loan was also to be rais­
ed by- the same party for the use of the Duke,
for which he was to obtain a considerable sum of
m

for which he was to obtain a considerable sum of
money from government for Col. French. The -
last case related to Capt Maling, who, through
the influence of Mrs Clarke, was appointed to
an envigney, and to a rank of Lieut, ai d Cap.,
he being all the while acting as clerk in a public
for the sake of form. Such were the charges, j
and with the statement of which Col. Wardle .
prefaced a motion for the appointment of a Se- I
lect Committee to enquire into the conduct of
the Commander-in-Chief. The charges were
treated a

the Commander-in-Chief. The charges were
treated as a seditious conspiracy, particularly by
Lord Ccstlereugh and the Ministerial Members.
The present Duke of Wellington was then a
Member of the House, and warmly espoused the
cause of. the Duke. .After some debate the1 .
House agreed to receive the evidence that might j
be adduced by the accuser in a committee of the -
whole House. The evidence was then gone |
through, the hearing of which occupied a con­
siderable portion of the time of the House.— !:
Mrs Clarke was a principal witness. On the 9th | i

Mrs Clarke was a principal witness. On the 9th | i
of March, the case being closed, Mr Wardle j ,
moved an address, in.which, after reciting sub-;.
star.tially the tnatm^of the evidence, and the :
.inferences to be drawibfrom it, he expressed an j-. >. opinion that tire Duke ought to be removed from 1
A jiiti;. 'Several .amendments were Proposed, one h
J !• y the Chancellor -p: the Exchequer, declar-L
•ng the^lhribcyn^m^ JL' .t
j,y Mr'i,
same time acknowledging bis great official servi­
ces. On the 17th, "the Chancellor of the Ex- L
che

ces. On the 17th, "the Chancellor of the Ex- L
chequer proposed some amendments. However '
while these debates were about to close, the ,
Duke waited on the King, and tendered his re- j
singnation in a written document, which com­
mences in these terms:—" The House of C< m- ]
mons, after a long and full investigation of the
merits of certain allegatiouSagajuat-him^jiassed- (
a resolution of bis innocence; he might now ap­
proach his Majesty, and venture to rende. to him ,
his resignation of the chief command of his Ma- J

his resignation of the chief command of his Ma- J
jesty's army, as he could no longer be suspected ,
of acting from any apprehension of the result, or ,
be accused of having shrunk from the extent of (
an inquiry, from which, painful as it had been, ,
he trusted he should appedf, even to those who (
had been disposed to condemn his conduct, to <
have met with the patience and firmness which (
nocence." Sir David Dundas was the success- j
or. In his retirement the Duke endeavored to ,
console himself in the society of his friends. .1.

console himself in the society of his friends. .1.. ,
the meantime the charge and the proceedings on j
it made a sensible impression. Thanks were •
forwarded from various places to Col. Wardle. •
Some of these addresses were characterised by
very strong language, and no doubt must have .
been to Col. Wardle more than a counterbalance
for the charges of Jacobinism, conspiracy, treat- ,
on, &c., which were levelled against him in and' /
Aut of the House. ' I am attacked,' said he, at (
^dinner given to him by the Livery of London: r
PI

^dinner given to him by the Livery of London: r
PI have been attacked as holding an improper in- J
jjmacy with the'principal witness (Mrs Clarke,) g
\vhom I never saw until I visited her with a
-view of availing myself of her testimony to sub­
stantiate the charges which I brought, for the
benefit of the country^ Against the Duke of York, o
For this purpose oMy, I became acquainted with c
her, and 1 plcd^^Mplf that this was the sole a
,cause of my kn^RMge of that lady.' Dinners e
in various places followed, at which Col. War- c
d

in various places followed, at which Col. War- c
die was invited, and his popularity appeared to 1
be increasing every day.
| The restoration of his Royal Highness to the t
command of the Army, was a measure that gave ii
gratification to the military, generally; and we g
have no doubt, from the manner in which he per- S
formed the duties of that office, that it was one
also very beneficial to the public service. In the year 1820 the Duchess of York died, o
after having endured a lingering and painful dis-

after having endured a lingering and painful dis- s
Shortly afterwards the Duke drew the atten- t
tion of the public towards him by a declaration 1
of his intention to sit and vote during the Queen's e
trial. Ilcr Majesty's advocate was induced to a
notice the circumstance; and, in alluding to his v
-Royal Highneft personally, taunted him with be- fl
ing the, object of a condemnatory resolution in t
1809, which.was proposed by Mr Bathurst, who, d
at the mornq^tahen he (Mr Brougham) was i
speaking,' as ^Member of that Cabinet, by which 1

speaking,' as ^Member of that Cabinet, by which 1
the Queen was prosecuted. The memorable «
speech which he delivered in Parliament on the
25th of April 1S25, on the subject of the Catho­
lic claims, has been the subject of so much com- r
mentary as to require no description in this place.
It is justly execrated by the liberal minded of t
every country, and remains a stain upon his t
memory which time can never obliterate. What-
ever may be the opinion of others on the subject t
of it; nothing we think more certain, than that <

of it; nothing we think more certain, than that <
it emanated from hinueiL la the early part of t d In consequence of a dispute already known to
e the public, bis Royal Highness the Duke of York, i
attended by Lord Rawdon, and Lieut.-flpfonel 1
,s Lennox, accompanied by the Earl of Winchel- '
- sea, met at Wimbledon common. The ground '
7 was measured at 12 paces, and both parties; were t
e to fire on a signal agreed oij. The signal being 1 given, Lieutenant-Colonel Lennox fired, and the •
" ball grazed his Royal Highness's curl; the Duke

" ball grazed his Royal Highness's curl; the Duke <
I, of York did not fire. Lieutenant-Colonel Len- ] i fired. Lord Rawdon said it was not the Duke's 1
intention to fire; his Royal Highness had come i
d out on Lieutenant-Colonel Lennox's desire to >
|. give him satisfaction, and'had no animosity a- <
gainst him. Lieutenant-Colonel .Lennox pressed I
. that the Duke of York should fire, which was <

NOTICE.
N TUESDAY the tenth inst. Josiah Lakin
and his family consisting of bis wife and four
.boys, left the town of Putnam, where they had a
lawful settlement, and took lodgings in the town
of Palermo, and tarried there till the Saturday
evening following, and absconded in the night— I t

evening following, and absconded in the night— I tbeir destination is unknown, but their evident
intention is to put the town lo as much expense as
' possible, which they have repeatedly threatened. This is therefore to forbid all persons harboring or
trusting, any of the said Lakin's, and we hope that
the Selectmen of towns, within the State of Maine,
will be very cautious how they indulge those fugi-
gative vagabonds in idleness that are able to earn
- their own bread. WILLIAM WITT, J Selectmen

dom*-*-*Such are the foundations on which the edifice of

companied with a bnnch of grapes or an apple.
Thus she engaged the affection of many a little
heart,) but seemed absorbed in meditation; and
j you might often have observed her hands uplifted,
in the attitude of prayer. One day, after having ,
observed her. as she came, I asked her how she
could bring such heavy loads, old as she was;
and

could bring such heavy loads, old as she was;
and feeble. '' O," said she, " when I get great
load, then 1 go pray God give me strength to
carry it. So I go on, thinking all the way how
good God is, give his only Son die for poor sin­
ner ; think how good Jesus be, suffer so much
for such poor creature ; how good Holy Spirit
was, come into my bad heart, make it all new:
so these sweet thoughts make my mind so full
joy, I never think how heavy sand be on my old
back." (Here, said. I to my heart, learn how to
ma

back." (Here, said. I to my heart, learn how to
make thy heavy load of iron cares easy.) One day she passed with a bag of sand. On
her return she called on me; I enquired how
•much Mrs. — gave her for the sand. She was
unwilling to tell, and I feared she was unwilling
lest I should withhold my accustomed mite, on i account of what she had already received; I
. therefore insisted she should-let me see. She at
length consented, and I drew from the bag a
bone, not containing meat enough for half a
meal. Is this all ? Did that rich woman turn
you o

meal. Is this all ? Did that rich woman turn
you off so ? How cruel, how hard-hearted, I
exclaimed! '' Misse," she replied, " this made
me 'fraid let you see it; I 'fraid you would be
angry j I hope she have bigger heart next time,
iuirfy she forget now, that Jesus promise to pay
her all she give Sarah, Don't be angry, I pray
God to give her a great deal bigger heart."—
The conviction, that she possessed in an emi­
nent degree the spirit of Him, who said, " bfess
them that curse you," and prayed for his mur­
dere

them that curse you," and prayed for his mur­
derers, rushed upon my mind with energy, and
I could compare myself in some measure to
those who said, " shall we command fire to come
down from Heaven,'' &c. I think I never felt
deeper self-abhorrence and abasement: I left
her for a moment, and from the few comforts I
possessed, gave her a considerable portion. She
received them with the most visible marks of
gra&ude—arose to depart, went to the door,
and then turned, looking me in the face with
evident concern. Sarah, said I, what would
you hav

evident concern. Sarah, said I, what would
you have ? (supposing she wanted something I
had not thought of, and feared to ask.) " 0 my
good Misse,', said she, " nothing, only 'fraid
your big heart feel some proud, 'cause you give
This faithfulness, added to her piety and grati­
tude, completed the swell of feeling already
rising in my soul, and bursting into tears, I
may Have a bigger heart, don't forget to pray
that 1 may have a humbler one. I Will, Misse, I
will," she exclaimed with joy, and hastened on
her way. (Another excellence in her character,
was

her way. (Another excellence in her character,
was, that she loved the habitation of God's house,
and often appeared there, when from bad weath­
er or other causes, many a seat of afSuehce was
empty. She was always early, ever clean and
whole in her apparel, though sometimes almost
as much diversified with patches as the shep-
herd?s coat. She Was very <dd an<£ quite fee­
ble, yet she generally stood during public ser­
vice, with her eyes rivetted on the preacher. I ha

vice, with her eyes rivetted on the preacher. I have sometimes overtook her on the steps,
after service, and tapping her on her shoulder,
would say, have yon had a good day, Sarah ?
" AM good ; sweeter than honey," she would re­ In the spring of 1818, it was observed by her
friends that she did not appear at meeting as
usual, and one of her particular female benefac­
tors asked her the reason; when she with
streaming eyes told her, that her clothes had
become so old and ragged that she could not
come with comfort of decency; but said she
had bee

come with comfort of decency; but said she
had been praying God to provide for her in this
respect, a great dditle, and telling Jesus how
much she wanted to go to his house of prayer,
and expressed a strong desire to be resigned
and submissive to bis will. This was soon com-:
municated to a few friends, who promptly obey­
ed the cal, of Providence, and soon furnished
this suffering member of Christ, with a very decent
siiit of apparel. This present was almost over­
powering to her grateful heart. She received
them

powering to her grateful heart. She received
them as from the hand of her heavenly Eat her
kind Redeemer, in answer to her spectai* ira/er. But this did hot in the least diminish
ter gratitude to her benefactors; but said she
would go on, tell Jesus how good his dear peo­
ple was to this poor old creature, and pray her
good Father to give them great reward. Two of the garments given her, she received
with every mark of joy. On being asked why
she set so high a value on these, she replied, " O, '1
these just what I pray for so long, so to lay out

these just what I pray for so long, so to lay out
my poor old body, clean and decent, like God's i quested a friend to keep for her, fearing to carry
them home, lest they should be taken from her. i She was however, persuaded tb wear one of
them to meeting, upon condition that if she in­
jured that, another should be provided ; the , other was preserved by her friend, and made
use of at her death. Thus, was this humble band of female friends'-
honored, by anointing, as it were, the body, be»
forehand; to the fcurial. And 1 doubt not but
that

forehand; to the fcurial. And 1 doubt not but
that her prayer was heard, and will be answered
in their abundant reward. The last visit I had
from her was tin the summer of 1818. She
had attended a funeral, and returning, called at
my< cottage. She complained of great Weari­
ness, and pain in hep limbs, and showed me her
feet, which were much swollen. I inquired the
cause: " 0," said she, with a serene 9mile,
" Death come creeping on.' I think in grave­
yard to-day, Sarah must lie here soon;" Well,
are you willing to die ? do you feel ready ? ** O,

are you willing to die ? do you feel ready ? ** O,
I hope, Misse, if my bad heart,4ell trite, I
willing and ready to dn just as Jesus bid me ; if
he say you must die, I glad to be with him ; if
he say, live and suffer great deal more, then I
willing do that; I think Jesus know best. Some­
time I get such look of . heaven, I long to go see
Jesus; see happy anget, see holy saint—throw
I away my bad heart, lay down my bid body, and
go where I no sin. then I tell Jesus; he say,
Sarah, I prepare a place for you, then I come
take

Sarah, I prepare a place for you, then I come
take you to myself. Then 1 be quite like chihi,
don't want to go till he call me." Much more
she said upon this interesting subject; which in­
dicated a soul ripe for heavenly glories. Whets
we parted, I thought it very doubtful whether we
ever met again below. In the course ot three
weeks from this time, I heard that Sarah was no
more ! Is Sarah dead;? said I: and the inquiry
gave rise, to the thoughts contained in the fol­
lowing lines. Is Sarah dead ? let not a sigh arise

lowing lines. Is Sarah dead ? let not a sigh arise.
To mourn her exit from this world of wo •
Rather let tears of joy suffuse the eyes
That oft-have wept her suffering slate below.
Is Sarah dead ? then those poor aged limbs
So long with pain and Weariness oppress'd,
An easy bed in yonder grave shall find,
" And long and sweet atall be the sacred rest."
Is Sarah dead ? then never, never more,
Shall hunger force her from her wretched cot
With eager step, a morsel to implore,
Where poverty arid tears are heeded not.
No longer

Where poverty arid tears are heeded not.
No longer bent beneath i heavy fo id,
I see her%truggle on h4r weary way,
With lifted hands, imploring strength of God
To bear the heat and burden of the day; That untaught mind shall now lament no more
Its scanty knowledge of God's holy word • Or grieve that she had riot begun before '
To banquet on the goodness of the Lord.
I lov'd thee, Sarah; for I Well could trace
My Saviour's image on thy humble soul •
Your heart the seat of his" almighty grace.
And every action prov'd its sweet control*
O happy

And every action prov'd its sweet control*
O happy Sarah ! though so poor and low,
That few on thee would cast a pitying look, s
Since thy Redeemer deign'd his love to show,
Arid wrote thy name in life's immortal book:
And rather for, would I thy triumphs share.
(And ere the triumph all thy sorrows feel,)
Than gain the laurel earthly Conqu'rors wear
And all the sceptres kings and princes vfeld.
Thus, while the pen of many a ready writer
is employed in imparting instruction, reproof,
or correction, to the rising, or risen generation:

or correction, to the rising, or risen generation:
while the deeds of the mighty are recorded with
splendor, the exploits of the heroes proclaimed
(ke house-tops, and the - virtues arid chari­
ties of God's people are exhibited, that others
may see their good works and glorify their Father
who is iri. heaven, I would, according to iriy
humble ability, snatch from oblivion the example
of one, who, though scorned by .the proud, arid
overlooked by the great, yet was known and
beloved by a humble few, and by them the grace

Christian Register: Vol. 8, No. 34, - August 22, 1829 Follow this and additional works at: https://digitalmaine.com/skowhegan_history_house_newspapers these, and many other observances' were
founded On. mere tradition. Therefore it is, that our Lord in refuting
the errors of the people, so frequently op­
poses his instructions to these * traditions of t

poses his instructions to these * traditions of t r
the elders.' 'Ye have heard that it was said
' by them of old time, or by the ancients':' or,
as some translate it, * to them of old time.
But I say unto you.* Arid'it was doubtless?
this superior dignity and authority, as well
as the simplicity and grandeur of the truths,
he uttered, that astonished his own- country­
men; and made even the Roman officers ex­
cuse themselves to the Pharisees for not ap­
prehending him,' by. confessing the power of
his words, ' Never man spake tike this man.1 , MU.

his words, ' Never man spake tike this man.1 , MU.1 HANNAH E1ND8EY.
In our last Register, we presented to our readers
gart of. a memoir .of this exceltept Lady, by her friend
Mrs.- Newcome Cappe. But our limits compelled us
with her husband, she quitted the scenes, whi^h time
and habit, and numerons friendships had made deK<«o
them, resigning their .temporal prospects within thex*
church, of which he had for many years been the faith­
ful minister, to entgr upon untried and arduous labors,
which involved, as will be seen, the most painful

which involved, as will be seen, the most painful sac­
rifices. Of her sentiments and conduct at this period, Mr. '
Belsham, the successor of Mr. Lindsey in Essex
Street Chapel, and- for many years her affectionate
friend, as well as pastor, thus writes in. a sermon
preached on the occasion of her death. But the great trial now approached. Mr.
Lindsey, after a struggle of ten years to rec­
oncile his mind to continue in a profession,
to which lie had from his infancy been de­
voted, for which he'had been educated,'and
to the

voted, for which he'had been educated,'and
to the duties of which he was fondly attach­
ed, in the exercise of which he possessed
every earthly comfort, was universally res­
pected, affectionately beloved, and eminent­
ly useful, his ingenuous mind being no long­
er able to dissemble to itself the gross im­
propriety of conducting tr form of public
worship, and of constantly rehearsing the
most solemn invocations in which the minis-'
ter meant one thing and the congregation
another, came at length to the virtuous and
honora

another, came at length to the virtuous and
honorable resolution of resigning his prefer­
ment for the sake of truth and'-'a good con­
science.
. 1 ' I '
This was-d severe trial: and that circum­
stance which_gave the greatest Uneasiness
c»«nro»ebr was the
thought of reducing IHB fleWTetl mm fifth- "
ion of his life and fortunes, who had hither­
to lived in respectability and affluence, arid
who had experienced none of the hard­
ships, of a straitened income, to a state little
short of absolute indigence. For, it having emplar

short of absolute indigence. For, it having emplary parochial minister io lay out for the
benefit of his parishoners the whole of that
income which he received from them, noth­
ing was left for future subsistence but the
small sum which he might - be enabled to
raise from the sale of the larger part of a
valuable library, and a small quantity of
family plate. * And though they could hard­
ly expect to have been deserted by so large
a proportion of those who in the day of pros­
perity called themselves friends, it was at
the sa

perity called themselves friends, it was at
the same time out of the reach of all calcu­
lation to divine, that they should in so short
a time meet with such liberal and effectual
support from persons of high respectability
and affluence, whose very names were at
that time unknown. • The prospect was altogether dark and
doubtful. And it would, have been par­
donable, if female sensibility and delicahy
had fainted under the trial. A person whose
principles were less deeply rooted than thosp i of

principles were less deeply rooted than thosp i of Mrs. Lindsey, whose intellect was less
vigorous and whose fortitude was less firm,
would probably have been overcome in the
conflict; and by the moving indications of - regret and despondency if not by the bitter
i. epithets of irritation and reproach, she might , pion of truth. Very different were the tem­
per and conduct wf this Christian heroine, i then in the zenith of her days and in the se-
' cure possession of all that could render life
• useful or desirable. Far from expressing
, disap

• useful or desirable. Far from expressing
, disapprobation'of this extraordinary deterini-
' nation, far from exerting her influence-to dis- i step which would at once reduce him from af­
fluence' to poverty, and would expose him to
) the scorn and indignation of the unpririci-
I pled and unfeeling, she at oncee entered into purpose of his heart, she encouraged,- corti-
) forted, animated him, and if possible even
- went before him in the career of Ghris-
f tian glory. ' She is, has been, and will Jie
r a great comfort and support, and will act
r this

r a great comfort and support, and will act
r this, as every oilier part, like a true Chris-
r tian, for such only she seeks to become.' 1 So writes this virtuous confessor .to an
- iptimate friend a few -weeks previous to
3 the -resignation of his living. And though
- in addition to the sacrifice of affluence for
; poverty, and of society for desertion, it
l cost her dear»to quit the vicinity. of an in-
t dulgent mother, and to incur the reseflt- > ment, for so it proved, of him to whom she
, had been accustomed to look with filial
- venera

, had been accustomed to look with filial
- veneration, she made light of .all, and with a
i magnanimity of spirit which Christian prin-
; ciples only can inspire, in the depth of win- sions, take Their rise, and derive their char-' the:
acter from our thoughts. All the virtuous f0U!
actions which have adorned and blest socie- *]
ty, and all the deeds of crime which have the
polluted and cursed it, sprang at first from p'os
sorpe casual and trivial thought-which- re- the
quired only a trifling exertion to repress or" by

quired only a trifling exertion to repress or" by i
to cherish. How few are tho.wicked actions as s
to which any individual has been driven by Bu/
the ovewhelming attack of unlooked; for this
temptation, or for which he was not gradu- as (
ally prepared by an unregulated and disor- he i
dered- imagination. By dwelling in thought mei
oh a deed of wickedness, or by permitting it 'cus
to rise often before the imagination, the ah- pre!
horrence of wickedness is- lessened , the; con- his
stancy of good resolutions is shaken; arid the ==

stancy of good resolutions is shaken; arid the ==

and-principles, though in some respects pe- I

-ones shall have its reward.'

What should the cloud be made of ? blessed child !

What should the cloud be made of ? blessed child !
Thy spirit, borne upon a breeze of joy,
All day bath ranged through sunshine, clear yet mild; And now thou tiemblest! Wherefore? in thy soul
Theie lies no past, no future. Thou hast heard
The sound of presage from the distance roll,
Thy breast bears traces of no arrowy word: From ifiee no love hath gone ; thy young mind's eye • Hath look'd not into death's, and thence become
A questioner of mute eternity,
A weary searcher for a viewless home : Nor hath th

A weary searcher for a viewless home : Nor hath thy sense been quickened into pain,
By feverish watching for some step beloved ;
Free are thy thoughts, an ever changeful train,
Glancing like dew-drops and as lightly moved. Yet now; on billows of strange passion tossed,
How art thou wilder'd in the cave of sleep !
My gentle child! midst what dim phantoms lost,
Thus in mysterious anguish dost thou weep! Awake! they sadden me—those early tears,
First gushings of the strong dark river's flow
That must o'ersweepthy soul with coming years ; Fo

That must o'ersweepthy soul with coming years ; Forcing wild spray-drops bj»t'lrom childhood's eyes!

Wher» now thy thoughts "dismay'd and darkling rove;

The home still bright for thee with guardian love ! Happy, fair child ! that yet a mother's voice
Cob win thee back from visionary strife!

Start from the dream-like wilderness of life ? INF

Start from the dream-like wilderness of life ? INFANT SCHOOL.
A small jjainplilet of 16 pages 18 mo. has just been
printed, bearing the title of ' The First Annnal Report
of the Boston Infant School Society.' The Report is
brief, and is copied below. Appended to it is a letter
from one of the teachers, giving a sketch of the mode
of instruction &c., and containing several anecdotes il­
lustrative of the beneficial effects of the school upon
the children and their parents.. The length of this let­
ter alone prevents our transferring it to our colu

ter alone prevents our transferring it to our columns.
Wo think no one can read it without a conviction ot
th* utility of the school, and of the soundness of the
views of those to whose care it is entrusted. The |
school is at present kept in Atkinson Street, and, we
learn from this Repoit, may be visited on Tuesday ,,
Wednesday, Thursday and Friday mornings from 9 to
11 o'clock, and in these hours only.
The design aud plan of the Infant school '
belonging to this Society were suggested by 1
a small number of ladies in the spring of 1
known,

a small number of ladies in the spring of 1
known, funds sufficient (e justify them in 1
commencing the undertaking were collected I
without difficulty. Tho Society was organ- 1
ized and the insfracters appointed in May. 1
Owing to. the difficulty.^ol procuring rooms f
the school was not opened till near tho close !
of June; although all things wore in readi- .
fless a month previous.
The school began with seventeen schol­
ars; before the end of the first month the
-number had increased to more than sixty, a a
larg

-number had increased to more than sixty, a a
large part of whom were under two years of 1 school commenced, though tho best that 1
could nt the time bo procured, were not well 1
suited to tho object. The want of space ?
was if circumstance very much to be regret- ,
ted, as some of the most'useful and interest­
ing exercises usual in Infant schools were
necessarily either altogether omitted, or but £
imperfectly performed. And vet,notwithstand­
ing this and other obstacles the principal ob- •
jectu of an Infant school were accomplished, j
Man

jectu of an Infant school were accomplished, j
Many indigent parents were relieved of tho c
care of very young, add (in consequence of- t
ten of bad management) very troublesome
children, during the whole day. The chil- c
drbn, meantime, were not only preserved
sources of vexation and discomfort, but were
made happy, while their-powers of body and
mind, above all, their moral, nature, were {
developed and improved. It may seem in- a
credible to persons much acquainted with j
children, especially the children .of the poor, j

children, especially the children .of the poor, j
that so great a number, mere infants, should j
bo .kept happy and - occupied—during the
whole day, that scarcely one is heard to cry ,c
for hours, and that two or three individuals
(extremely well qualified, we must allow, .
and devoted to the task) are able to produce
such effects. 'Yet all thia is true, as is at- .
tested by the monthly reports of the Mana­
gers, who visit the school regularly, as well 1
aa by visitors who have attended it. The school .p

aa by visitors who have attended it. The school .proposes to be nothing more v
than a large nursery regulated with wisdom f
and kindness. The moral character of the
children is the principal object of attention. 1
Knowledge can be acquired at almost any |
period of life, but the moral character may
bp, and often is, unalterably stamped at a '
very early age. .It is in their moral and re­
ligious edacation that the children of the '
poor are most likely to be neglected; to sup- .
* ply this deficiency is the main object of this
s

* ply this deficiency is the main object of this
school. The children are kept under as '
little restraint as is consistent with order, (
that they may exhibit their real dispositions
and propensities. Some intellectual mstruc- 1
tion is given, ahbeugh this is a secondary
object; occupation, amusement, and* faaental ;
discipline are thus furnished, and the little '
knowledge that is acquired, (which of course 1
cannot and ought not to be much at this ear­
ly age) is of a preliminary and useful kind.

than hellish malice. Or he may' have the

every instance there is a sense of .oppression
and helplessness ; 'and the extent to which
these are carried varies according to the vio- .
lence of the paroxysm. The individual nev­
er feel

lence of the paroxysm. The individual nev­
er feels himself a free agent ; on the contra­
ry he is spell-bound by some enchantment,
and remains an unresisting victim for malice'
to work its will upon. He can neither'
breathe, nor walk, nor run with wonted facil-
ity. If pursued by any imminent danger' he
can hardly drag one limb after another ; if
engaged in combat, his blows are utterly in­
effective ;• if involved in the fangs of any ant-'
mal or in the grasp of an enemy, extrication
is Impossible ; he struggles, he pants, he
toils,

is Impossible ; he struggles, he pants, he
toils, but it is all in Vain ; his muscles are
rebels to the will, and refuse to obey its calls.
In no case is there- a sense of any freedom5;
the benumbing stupor never departs'from him.
and his whole being is locked up in one migh-
ty spasm. Sometimes he is forcing himself
through an aperture too-small for the recep­
tion of his body, and is there arrested and tor
tured by the pangs of suffocation", produced-
by the pressure to which he is exposed ; or
he loses 'his way in a narrow labyrinth, and
gets

he loses 'his way in a narrow labyrinth, and
gets involved in its constructed and inextri­
cable mazes ; or he is entombed alive in a
sepulchre, beside the mouldering dead.—-
There is, in most cases, an intense reality in
all that he sees, of hears, or fdels. The as­
pect of the hideous • phantoms which harass
his imagination is bold .rand defined ; the
sounds which greet his ear appallingly dis- f
tinct; and when any 'dimness* or confusion of

headline_text: Remarque sur la surface du quatrième ordre possédant un point double inflexionnel
text:
REMARQUE SUR LA SURFACE
DU QUATRIÈME ORDRE
POSSÉDANT UN POINT DOUBLE
INFLEX1ONNEL Sommaire. — La surface quartique ayant pour seule singularité un point double
inflexionnel est l'image d'une involution du second ordre appartenant à une sur­
face de genre p" = P4 = 1, dont on construit une image projective dans S'J.
Détermination des trois homographies 2 à 2 permutables qui conservent cette
surface. La lecture de deux notes récentes de MM. M. Dedo et
B. D'orgeval (') nous a remis en mémoire des reche

B. D'orgeval (') nous a remis en mémoire des recherches déjà
anciennes sur les involutions du second ordre n'ayant qu'un nombre
fini de points unis, appartenant à une surface de genres pa — P4 — 1 (2)
Nous nous proposons de démontrer dans cette note <\u'une surface du
quatrième ordre possédant comme seule singularité un point double inflexionnel,
est l'image d'une involution du second ordre appartenant à une surface de genres Pa = P, = 1.
Nous déterminerons en outre une image projective de la surface
contenant cette involution. (1) M. Dedo, Una class

contenant cette involution. (1) M. Dedo, Una classica superficie del quarto ordine ed eleganti questioni ad essa collegate
(Periodico di Matematiche, 1953, pp. 104-128, 176-185, 207-228).
B. d'ORGEVAL, A propos des surfaces du 4e ordre possédant des points doubles inflexionnels
(Publications scientifiques de l'Université d'Alger, série A, Sciences mathématiques,
1954» volume I, pp. 307-311).
(2) L. Godeaux, Mémoire sur les surfaces algébriques doubles ayant un nombre fini
de points de diramation (Annales de la Faculté des

de points de diramation (Annales de la Faculté des Sciences de Toulouse, 1914,
pp. 289-312). i. La surface 'I>, du quatrième ordre, possédant un point double
inflexionnel à l'infini sur l'axe des z, sans autre singularité, a une équa­
tion de la forme
où/2 = o,/4 =o sont respectivement les équations d'une conique et
d'une quartique que nous supposerons générales et se coupant en huit
points distincts. Cette surface est birationnellement équivalente à un
plan double dont la courbe de diramation se compos

plan double dont la courbe de diramation se compose des courbes
A = o, f, = o.

sentent une surface F transformée en soi par trois homographies
harmoniques
deux à deux permutables. Ces homographies engendrent sur F une
involution I4, d'ordre quatre, composée au moyen de trois involutions
du second ordre :

étant de genres pa = P4 = i, la surface F est également de genres
Pa — P* = I. 2. Dans la correspondance (1,2) entre

Pa — P* = I. 2. Dans la correspondance (1,2) entre les surfaces <ï> et F, la courbe
de diramation sur <ï> est constituée par les huit droites communes aux droites que nous désignerons par <j" a2,
Les sections planes T, de la surface <I> rencontrent ces droites en
huit points; elles sont de genre trois donc il leur correspond sur F
des courbes C, de genre neuf. Le modèle projectif de F dont les courbes
C, sont des sections hyperplanes est donc une surface d'ordre 16, à
courbes C, sections hyperplanes de genre 9, appart

courbes C, sections hyperplanes de genre 9, appartenant à un espace Su
à neuf dimensions.
Sur cette surface, l'involution L est déterminée par une homogra­
phie harmonique, homologue de T et que nous désignerons encore
par T, ayant deux axes ponctuels |3, Ç6, respectivement de dimensions
trois et cinq. Les courbes C, sont découpées sur F par les hyperplans
passant par ç:; et cet espace coupe F aux huit points unis de I2. Les
courbes découpées par les hyperplans passant par Çg, courbes que nous
désignerons par C0, forment un système de dimensio

désignerons par C0, forment un système de dimension cinq, apparte­
nant à I2, mais dépourvu de points-base.
Les courbes r", qui correspondent sur 4» aux courbes C0, satisfont

rencontrant pas les droites a,, <r2, •••, a8 en des points variables, c'est-à-

Les courbes ro sont de genre cinq, ce qui résulte d'ailleurs du fait que 4>
est de genres pa — P4 = 1 et qu'elles forment un s

est de genres pa — P4 = 1 et qu'elles forment un système de dimension
Sur le plan double équivalent à <i>, les courbes F0 sont représentées
par les coniques doubles et les courbes T, par les quartiques passant par les huit points de diramation et touchant/4 = o en quatre
points. 3. Revenons à la surface F d'ordre 16 de S0. Nous désignerons
par G2, G4 les courbes qui correspondent sur cette surface à la conique
fi = o et à la quartique/4 = o.
Aux droites doubles du plan double équivalent à «F, ou, si l'on
préfère, aux sections de F par les plans passant p

préfère, aux sections de F par les plans passant par O, correspondent sur
F des courbes D0 d'ordre huit et de genre trois. Les courbes D0 appar­
tiennent à un système complet |D|, de dimension trois, par consé­
quent chaque courbe D, ou D0, appartient à un espace linéaire à cinq
dimensions. Les courbes D0 rencontrent la courbe G2 en quatre points,
car à G2 correspond sur F la courbe infiniment petite constituant le
domaine du point O. Désignons cette courbe par A,.
Si nous appelons A0 les courbes qui correspondent

Si nous appelons A0 les courbes qui correspondent sur F aux courbes

graphie harmonique ayant pour éléments unis le système |D0| et la

En rapportant projectivement les courbes D aux plans de l'espace,
on obtient comme modèle projectif de F la quadrique double F2 ayant
comme courbe de diramation la section de cette qua

comme courbe de diramation la section de cette quadrique par /\ = o.
Aux coniques du plan des x, y correspondent sur la quadrique F2
les sections de celle-ci par les cônes du second ordre de l'espace.
Ces sections appartiennent au système complet, de dimension huit, dé­

appartient à |C| et est le lieu des points unis de

appartient à |C| et est le lieu des points unis de I2.
L'homographie harmonique T2 est donc une homologie de centre
çô et d'hyperplan Comme les couples de courbes D0 forment des
courbes du système de dimension 8 qui vient d'être envisagé, le point 4. Il nous reste à déterminer les axes de l'homographie harmo­
nique T,.
On sait que si l'on projette une surface cubique d'un de ses points
sur un plan, le contour apparent est une quartique générale (Théorème
de Geiser). On peut donc prendre pour modèle proje

de Geiser). On peut donc prendre pour modèle projectif de la surface
F, une surface cubique (générale) T", le centre de projection étant un
point P de cette surface. Aux coniques du plan des x, y correspondent les sections de la
surface Y par les cônes du second ordre de sommet P. Ces courbes
appartiennent au système complet de dimension six des courbes
découpées sur T par les quadriques touchant cette surface en P.
Appelons H ces courbes, et observons que la section H0 de 'F par le
cône projetant/,. = o de P est une courbe H, car c

cône projetant/,. = o de P est une courbe H, car c'est l'intersection
de 'F avec la quadrique polaire de P. D'autre part, la section H' de Y
par le cône projetant f2 = o de P appartient également à |H|. H'
est la courbe de diramation dans la correspondance entre <t>, et F,
elle correspond à la courbe G2.
D'après la théorie des homographies permutables, dans S," le point
;ô, l'espace £5 et le plan Ç, intersection des espaces 53 et ÇJ, appartien­
nent certainement aux axes de T,. Or, la courbe Gv

nent certainement aux axes de T,. Or, la courbe Gv est une courbe C
qui ne contient pas comme partie la courbe G2, donc T, possède comme
axes le plan et l'espace déterminé par le point ÇJ et l'espace Ç8.
La courbe G2 étant une courbe D, appartient à un espace linéaire
à cinq dimensions, situé dans il rencontre suivant un espace à
quatre dimensions, coupant G2 en huit points. Ces points appartien­
nent à la courbe G, et sont les points unis de l'involution I2.
Il existe un hyperplan contenant le plan ç2 et l'e

Il existe un hyperplan contenant le plan ç2 et l'espace à cinq dimen­
sions de G,. A la section de F par cet hyperplan correspond sur 'F
la courbe H' qui intervient ici comme courbe H.
Aux sections de F par les hyperplans passant par Çg correspondent
sur 'F la courbe H' augmentée des sections de 'F par les plans passant
par P.
En résumé, la surface F, de genres pa = P, = 1, de Sa, est transformée
en soi par trois homographies harmoniques deux à deux permutables :
Une homographie possédant deux axes ponctuels /, Ç

headline_text: Sur les surfaces algébriques ayant une courbe double tracée sur une surface du quatrième ordre
text:
Extrait du Bulletin de la Société royale des Sciences de Ltége.
Séance du 21 février 1946. — N» 2. Sur les surfaces algébriques ayant une courbe double
tracée sur une surface du quatrième ordre,
par Lucien GODEAUX, Membre de la Société. Notre but, dans cette note, est de construire des surfaces
algébriques dont la courbe double forme l'intersection complète
de la surface et d'une surface du quatrième ordre. La courbe
double possède un certain nombre de points triples, triples
également pour la surface, et ces points sont doub

également pour la surface, et ces points sont doubles pour la
surface du quatrième ordre. Si F est la surface d'ordre n que
l'on se propose de construire et D sa courbe double, il existe des
surfaces d'ordre n inscrites dans la surface du quatrième ordre
le long de la courbe D. Nous sommes ainsi conduit à utiliser
les résultats de G. Humbert sur la surface de Kuramer (*) et
les nôtres sur la surface-enveloppe d'une série simplement
infinie de quadriques, d'indice deux, ayant huit points-base
distincts (formant un groupe de Lamé). On sait qu'

distincts (formant un groupe de Lamé). On sait qu'une telle
surface représente une involution du second ordre appartenant
à une surface intersection de trois hyperquadriques de l'espace
linéaire à cinq dimensions (2).
Nous nous bornons à indiquer la valeur du genre géométrique
des surfaces trouvées. Ces surfaces sont évidemment régulières. 1. Soit F une surface algébrique d'ordre n> 4 possédant
comme seules singularités une courbe double D d'or

comme seules singularités une courbe double D d'ordre 2a et t points triples à la fois pour la surface et pour la courbe D.
En un de ces points triples, nous supposerons que les trois
tangentes à la courbe D sont les arêtes d'un trièdre proprement
dit, ce qui est le cas général.
